  1. Therapeutic Areas
  2. Oncology

Oncology

Therapeutic Area Guide for Real World Analytics & Accelerators (RWAA)

Published

April 6, 2026

Why Oncology Matters to RWAA

Oncology is one of Lilly’s fastest-growing Therapeutic Areas, anchored by a portfolio of targeted therapies and a pipeline spanning breast cancer, lung cancer, colorectal cancer, biliary tract cancer, and other solid tumors. The oncology RWD landscape is distinct from other TAs — driven by rapid treatment evolution, biomarker-defined patient subgroups, and a regulatory environment increasingly receptive to real-world evidence for label support and post-market commitments.

RWAA supports Oncology across the full evidence lifecycle — from epidemiologic characterization of biomarker-defined populations, through treatment pattern and sequencing analyses in a fast-moving competitive market, to regulatory-grade RWE studies supporting label maintenance and expansion.

Disease Areas & RWD Landscape

Breast Cancer

Breast cancer is Lilly’s largest oncology franchise, anchored by CDK4/6 inhibitors (abemaciclib, Verzenio®) in HR+/HER2- disease. RWD in breast cancer is relatively mature, with strong claims and oncology-specific EHR databases available.

Key RWD considerations:

  • HR/HER2 biomarker status is not reliably captured in claims; oncology EHR databases (Flatiron) with structured pathology data are required for biomarker-stratified analyses
  • Stage at diagnosis (early vs. metastatic) is critical for cohort construction; claims-based staging algorithms have limited accuracy — Flatiron or SEER linkage preferred
  • Line of therapy (LOT) construction in metastatic breast cancer requires careful rules for gap allowances and combination regimens
  • Adjuvant vs. metastatic settings have distinct data needs; adjuvant analyses benefit from longer follow-up and cancer registry linkage for recurrence endpoints

Lung Cancer

Lung cancer — particularly non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) — is a high-priority area driven by biomarker-directed therapies. Lilly’s selpercatinib (Retevmo®) targets RET-altered NSCLC and thyroid cancer.

Key RWD considerations:

  • RET, EGFR, ALK, KRAS and other driver mutation data are not available in claims; Flatiron and similar oncology EHR databases with structured genomic data are essential
  • Biomarker testing patterns and turnaround time are themselves high-value research questions for Medical Affairs
  • Small, biomarker-defined populations limit sample sizes — multi-database or registry approaches may be needed
  • NSCLC staging (I–IV) requires structured EHR or registry; claims-based proxies are insufficient

Colorectal Cancer (CRC)

Colorectal cancer is a growing area within Lilly’s oncology portfolio. RWD in CRC is increasingly important for characterizing treatment patterns and unmet need across lines of therapy.

Key RWD considerations:

  • MSI/MMR and RAS/BRAF mutation status are critical for treatment eligibility and cohort definition; require oncology EHR with structured molecular data
  • Metastatic CRC (mCRC) LOT analyses are complex — regimens frequently include combinations of chemotherapy backbones with targeted agents
  • Liver metastasis status and resectability are clinically important but not well-captured in claims
  • Sidedness (right vs. left colon primary) is prognostically important but requires pathology or structured EHR data

Biliary Tract Cancer (BTC)

Biliary tract cancer — including cholangiocarcinoma and gallbladder cancer — represents a rare tumor type with high unmet need. Lilly’s portfolio includes therapies targeting FGFR and IDH alterations.

Key RWD considerations:

  • Small patient populations require multi-database pooling or cancer registry linkage (SEER, NCDB) to achieve adequate sample sizes
  • BTC subtypes (intrahepatic vs. extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma vs. gallbladder cancer) are differentiable by ICD-10 but require careful algorithm validation
  • FGFR2 fusion and IDH1 mutation data are not in claims; Flatiron or Foundation Medicine genomic linkages are typically required
  • Treatment patterns in BTC are rapidly evolving — historical RWD may not reflect current standard of care

Other Solid Tumors

Lilly’s oncology pipeline continues to expand into additional tumor types. Common RWD challenges across rare and emerging indications include small sample sizes, evolving treatment standards, and limited biomarker data availability in traditional data sources.

Common RWAA Study Types in Oncology

Common Oncology Study Types
Study Type Typical Requestor Data Source(s) Notes
Treatment pattern / LOT analysis Medical Affairs, HEOR Flatiron, claims Line of therapy, sequencing, time on treatment
Comparative effectiveness (CER) HEOR, HTA Flatiron, claims External control arms increasingly common
Safety / pharmacovigilance GPS Claims, Flatiron AEs, dose modifications, treatment discontinuation
Epidemiology / disease burden Pipeline teams, Medical Affairs SEER, claims, Flatiron Incidence, prevalence, biomarker prevalence
Biomarker testing patterns Medical Affairs, Regulatory Flatiron, Foundation Medicine Testing rates, turnaround, result utilization
External control arm (ECA) Regulatory, Clinical Flatiron, registries Supports single-arm trials; high regulatory scrutiny
Budget impact / cost analyses Market Access, HEOR Claims Payer-facing; total cost of care in oncology
PRO / patient experience HEOR Survey, registry HRQoL, symptom burden, treatment tolerability
Real-world effectiveness (RWE) Regulatory, HEOR Flatiron, claims Label support, post-market commitments

Key Data Sources for Oncology

Oncology-Specific EHR Databases

  • Flatiron Health — the primary oncology RWD source at Lilly; structured EHR from ~280 oncology practices; includes LOT, biomarker results, staging, ECOG, and mortality data; Lilly has a data access agreement
  • ConcertAI — oncology EHR platform with structured clinical data; growing alternative to Flatiron for select tumor types
  • Veeva Oncology — structured oncology EHR; useful for certain tumor types and geographies

Genomic & Biomarker Databases

  • Foundation Medicine (FMI) — comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) data; linkable to Flatiron for biomarker-stratified analyses
  • Tempus — AI-enabled genomic and clinical data; useful for rare biomarker-defined populations
  • AACR GENIE — multi-institutional genomic registry; useful for biomarker prevalence and genomic landscape studies

Cancer Registries

  • SEER (NCI) — population-based cancer registry; gold standard for incidence, prevalence, and survival; linkable to Medicare claims
  • NCDB (ACS/CoC) — hospital-based registry with staging, treatment, and facility-level data; broader than SEER but no claims linkage
  • NPCR (CDC) — complements SEER for population-level epidemiology

Claims Databases

  • Optum Clinformatics — useful for HCRU, cost, and treatment patterns when biomarker data not required
  • SEER-Medicare — linked registry-claims database; excellent for elderly cancer populations with longitudinal follow-up
  • CMS Medicare — important for older oncology populations; linkable to SEER for outcomes research
Data Source Selection Tip

Oncology studies almost always require biomarker, staging, or LOT data not available in claims. Flatiron Health is the default starting point for most Lilly oncology RWD studies. Claims are supplementary — primarily useful for HCRU, cost analyses, and populations not well-captured in Flatiron (e.g., community settings outside Flatiron’s network).

Analytic Considerations

Cohort Design

  • Biomarker-defined cohorts are the norm in oncology; cohort entry should be anchored to biomarker test result date or treatment initiation, with careful handling of patients tested at different points in their disease course
  • Metastatic vs. early-stage analyses have fundamentally different data requirements and analytic designs — these should not be conflated
  • LOT construction requires pre-specified rules for: gap allowances between cycles, combination vs. sequential regimens, maintenance therapy, and rechallenge
  • Index date selection (diagnosis, metastatic diagnosis, LOT initiation) has major implications for bias — must be pre-specified and clinically justified

Confounding Control

  • Oncology populations are highly heterogeneous — ECOG performance status, prior lines of therapy, metastatic sites, and biomarker profile are critical covariates
  • Traditional propensity score methods apply, but small sample sizes in biomarker-defined populations often limit their use
  • Inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW) with stabilized weights is preferred when sample sizes allow
  • For external control arms, FDA guidance recommends pre-specification of estimand, covariate selection, and sensitivity analyses

Endpoint Ascertainment

  • Overall survival (OS) is the most reliable endpoint in oncology RWD; Flatiron uses a validated electronic death record linkage algorithm
  • Progression-free survival (PFS) and time to next treatment (TTNT) are common surrogates; TTNT is more reliable in claims, PFS requires structured EHR
  • Response rates (ORR, CR, PR) require structured oncologist-recorded data; available in Flatiron but with variable completeness
  • Adverse events are systematically undercaptured in EHR — claims-based AE algorithms or dedicated safety databases may supplement

External Control Arms (ECAs)

ECAs are a growing priority in Oncology RWAA, particularly for rare tumor types and single-arm trials seeking regulatory support. Key considerations:

  • FDA has issued specific guidance on ECAs — study design must be pre-specified and align with the estimand of the interventional trial
  • Flatiron is the most commonly accepted ECA data source by FDA for solid tumors
  • Contemporaneous controls (matched by calendar time to trial enrollment window) are strongly preferred
  • RWAA should engage Regulatory and Biostatistics early when ECAs are being considered

Regulatory-Grade RWE

Oncology is the most active TA for regulatory use of RWE at Lilly. Key requirements:

  • Pre-specified protocols and SAPs registered prior to data access
  • Transparent reporting per RECORD, STROBE, or FDA-recommended templates
  • Data quality documentation for Flatiron or other sources
  • Close collaboration with Regulatory Affairs and Global Statistical Sciences (GSS)

RWAA’s Role Across Oncology Functions

Key Collaborators & Contacts

Function Typical Ask to RWAA Notes
HEOR Disease burden, CER inputs, HRQoL linkage Closest collaboration; often co-lead studies
HTA Comparative effectiveness, ECA evidence, NMA inputs High demand as oncology portfolio expands
GPS AE patterns, safety signal evaluation, dose modifications Post-market commitments common in oncology
Medical Affairs LOT analyses, biomarker testing patterns, unmet need Frequently publication-oriented
Market Access Total cost of care, payer analyses, formulary support Oncology cost burden is a key payer concern
Regulatory ECA design and execution, RWE for label support Highest-rigor studies; engage early
Global Statistical Sciences ECA methodology, SAP review, regulatory alignment Required collaborator for regulatory-grade studies
